nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and tributaries over fine sand and leaf litter; highly tannin-stained water with very low conductivity and minimal dissolved solids

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (play sand or pool filter sand) — essential for barbel health
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) for tannins and microorganism growth
driftwood and twisted roots providing shaded hiding spots
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce surface brightness
gentle filtration — sponge filter or low-flow canister to replicate blackwater current

diet.notes: The Banner-Tail Corydoras forages along the substrate, sifting through sand with its barbels in search of food. Sinking wafers, micro-pellets, and bottom-feeding tablets work well as a staple diet, supplemented regularly with frozen or live bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. It will also scavenge detritus and leftover food from the tank floor. Because this species is most active around dusk and dawn (crepuscular), offering food after the aquarium lights go out improves the chances that the fish actually receives its share before faster tankmates consume it. A fine, smooth substrate is important for feeding health — coarse gravel or sharp aggregate can abrade the sensitive barbels and mouth, reducing feeding efficiency over time.

goodWith: other small peaceful blackwater fish (cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras, ember tetras)
dwarf cichlids compatible with soft acidic water (Apistogramma spp.) — with caution
other Corydoras species of similar size
blackwater-adapted dwarf shrimp (with size caveat — fry may be predated)
avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous tankmates that outcompete at feeding time
hard-water or alkaline-water fish incompatible with blackwater parameters
substrate-disturbing species that prevent fine-sand maintenance
fish known to nip fins of slow-moving bottom dwellers

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo summary page for Corydoras robineae (species ID 7785). Confirm individual image license before use.
Wikimedia Commons category for Corydoras robineae. Check individual image licenses; many Corydoras images are CC BY-SA.

summary: The Banner-Tail Corydoras (Corydoras robineae) is a blackwater catfish from Brazil’s upper Rio Negro, recognised by the bold black-and-white horizontal stripes across its caudal fin. It requires soft, acidic water, fine sand, and a group of at least six to thrive. A 2024 taxonomic revision proposes moving it to the genus Hoplisoma.
careNotes: Corydoras robineae is endemic to the upper Rio Negro drainage of Brazil, a classic Amazonian blackwater system characterised by extremely soft, acidic, tannin-rich water with negligible hardness and very low conductivity. Replicating these parameters is the central challenge — the species tolerates neutral pH in captivity but thrives and colours up best below pH 6.5, with GH under 6 dGH and minimal KH. Heavily buffered or hard tap water should be cut with RO or rain water and conditioned with Indian almond leaves or alder cones to lower pH and add humic acids. A fine sand substrate of at least 3 cm depth is essential: C. robineae uses its barbels to sift substrate constantly, and coarse gravel or sharp aggregate will abrade and erode the barbels within weeks, leading to secondary infection and death. Dim lighting suits this species, and dense floating plant cover or dried leaf litter helps replicate the shaded forest-floor conditions of its home habitat. A group of at least 6 (ideally 8–10) is strongly advised — this is a social species that displays more natural behaviour, forages more confidently, and spawns more readily in numbers. Sponge filtration or a low-flow canister outlet maintains gentle current without disturbing the fine substrate. Water changes of 20–30% weekly with matched temperature and chemistry are important, as sudden parameter swings can stress this soft-water species considerably. The dorsal and pectoral fin spines carry a mild venom capable of causing a sharp, stinging puncture wound — always use a soft net rather than bare hands when moving the fish. A 2024 taxonomic revision by Dias & Britto proposes moving C. robineae into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma; this reclassification is pending wider acceptance as of 2026.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is achievable but requires careful attention to water chemistry. A conditioning group with a ratio of 2:1 or 3:1 males to females is generally recommended, fed well with live and frozen foods in the weeks leading up to a spawning attempt. Spawning is often triggered by a significant cool water change — around 5–8°C cooler than the tank temperature — which mimics the onset of Amazonian rain season. Eggs are adhesive and are typically deposited on smooth broad-leaved plants, the aquarium glass, or driftwood surfaces. Eggs or adults should be separated promptly after spawning to prevent predation. At around 25°C, eggs generally hatch within approximately 3–5 days. The fry are initially very small and should be started on infusoria and vinegar eels or micro-worms before graduating to micro-pellets and baby brine shrimp as they grow. Maintaining blackwater conditions — tannin-stained, very soft, and acidic water — during the rearing period is widely considered to improve fry survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras robineae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (upper Rio Negro, Brazil), water parameters (temperature 23–28°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 1–8 dGH), adult size (~5–6 cm), minimum tank size, substrate requirements, diet, social behaviour (group of 6+), and breeding notes. Taxonomic note on pending Hoplisoma revision also cross-checked here.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras robineae
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 6.0 cm TL), native range (Rio Negro basin, Brazil), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), temperature range, and diet classification. FishBase lists species as Corydoras robineae Meinken, 1979.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide used for husbandry best practices: fine-sand substrate requirement, group size (6+), sinking food preference, venomous spine handling caution, and temperature range confirmation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras robineae species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for species identification notes, caudal fin banding description (bold black-and-white horizontal stripes — the diagnostic ‘banner’ character), blackwater biotope requirements, and intermediate care level classification.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Taxonomic revision placing Corydoras robineae in Hoplisoma
additional.notes: Reference for pending 2024 Dias/Britto taxonomic revision proposing reclassification of C. robineae into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. Acceptance of the new combination (Hoplisoma robineae) is not yet universal in the hobby literature as of 2026. FishBase synonymy page used as proxy citation; consult original Dias & Britto 2024 paper (Neotropical Ichthyology or equivalent) for full treatment.

fish.breedingNotes: Achievable in captivity with proper blackwater conditioning, a cool water-change spawning trigger, and a well-conditioned group. Eggs are adhesive and should be separated from adults promptly. Fry are very small and should be started on infusoria and micro-worms.
